entries.title: Portsmouth Housing Authority (PHA) — Overview
entries.body:
**Founded:** 1953. HUD-recognized high-performing agency.

**Scale:**
- 650+ apartments across 12 properties
- 1,700+ residents housed
- 460+ Housing Choice Vouchers (Section 8)

**Structure:** Two entities:
1. Portsmouth Housing Authority (PHA) — owns/operates
2. PHA Housing Development Ltd. — affiliated nonprofit, develops using LIHTC + CDFA credits

**Key contact:** Craig Welch, Executive Director — craigwelch@nh-pha.com / 603-436-4310

**Mission:** Quality, affordable housing for low- and moderate-income residents through fiscally responsible, creative organization.

entries.title: Key Projects
entries.body:
**Ruth Griffin Place**
- 82 units, ~$1,000/month rents
- Built on city-owned land with private capital + federal tax credits
- Tabor calls it 'the template for success' — proof that below-market housing can be built well in Portsmouth's core

**Sherburne School**
- 127 affordable units
- Former city-owned elementary school
- Originally proposed at 80-160 units, cut to 80-100 after Pannaway neighborhood pushback, then scrapped entirely before being revived
- Finally crossed finish line — Tabor's signature win

**Lafayette Road / Christ Church**
- 44 workforce housing units
- Also includes HAVEN domestic violence HQ + Little Blessings Childcare
- Funded partly by CDFA tax credits ($193k FY2026)
- Model for church/nonprofit surplus land partnerships

entries.title: Gateway Neighborhood Overlay District (GNOD)
entries.body:
What it is: Overlay district passed 2024 covering Commerce Way and Portsmouth Boulevard — formerly office-research zoned land.
What it allows:
	∙	Higher density housing and workforce housing
	∙	Up to 6 stories
	∙	Up to 120 dwelling units per building
	∙	Bonus incentives for affordability — developer can either include workforce units on-site OR transfer separate land to the city for the city/PHA to develop independently
Scale: ~23 acres, walkable to downtown, Market Basket, and Whole Foods

First major project under GNOD: Kane Company — 150 Portsmouth Blvd
	∙	274 market-rate apartments across three 6-story buildings on an 8.4-acre parcel
	∙	Technical Advisory Committee voted March 3, 2026 to advance to Planning Board for site plan approval
	∙	Buildings fronting Portsmouth Blvd with parking at rear; connected by landscaped outdoor amenity areas
	∙	South portion (grade change, buffer from Osprey Drive residences) kept undeveloped — walking paths and recreation space
	∙	Explicitly targeting “missing middle” tenants — teachers, firefighters, workforce earners; not a luxury-only play
	∙	Mix of unit sizes and rental rates planned
	∙	Kane Co. pursuing the land transfer density bonus — transferring separate property to the city (needs City Council approval) in exchange for the 6-story/120-unit-per-building allowance
Developer: The Kane Company. CEO Michael Kane. SVP Development Kimery Poldrack.

Significance: GNOD is now producing real projects. The Kane land transfer model is exactly the mechanism designed into the overlay — city gains a development parcel for PHA/nonprofit affordable housing without spending acquisition money. Tabor wants to replicate this overlay approach for other underutilized zones citywide.
Quote from Tabor: “Regardless of how it got here, the discussions led to a lot of innovative ideas and innovative zoning that’s going to allow us to boost the city housing stock in a location that’s not going to have a lot of neighborhood impact.”

entries.title: Housing Action Plan — Four Pillars
entries.body:
Framework Tabor described — council tasking committee to turn advocate recommendations into executable plan.

**1. Remove Zoning Barriers**
- Audit ordinance for parking minimums, setbacks, use restrictions, density caps
- Identify quick wins
- Feed into 2027 zoning rewrite
- Study Nashua and other NH cities on enabling legislation
- Build 'barriers register' — living doc tracking obstacles + status

**2. Put More Land Into Use**
- Inventory city-owned parcels (Sherburne = proof of concept)
- Identify surplus land from nonprofits/churches (Lafayette Rd = model)
- Map underutilized commercial/office zones (GNOD = template)
- Prioritize walkable sites

**3. Increase Density Strategically**
- Differentiate by neighborhood — higher near downtown/commercial, gentler (ADUs, duplexes) in residential like Pannaway
- ADU streamlining
- 50/50 market rate / affordable model (Montgomery County MD template)
- GNOD as replicable overlay tool

**4. Deploy Financial Tools**
- Tax abatements (sunset when affordability covenants expire)
- Tax deferrals for low-income homeowners
- LIHTC / CDFA credits via PHA Housing Development Ltd.
- Housing Trust Fund — ensure active deployment, push for dedicated staff
- HUD/Section 8 optimization — voucher utilization + landlord participation
- Benchmark against comparable Seacoast/NH markets

**Cross-cutting: Data & Accountability**
- Baseline metrics: units/year, waitlist trend, voucher utilization, cost/unit
- Demand signals: emergency assistance requests rose 28 → 81 between July–Sept 2024
- Regular public reporting

entries.title: Council Strategic Goals (Housing)
entries.body:
**Goal summary:** Increase supply, decrease cost, simplify process, expand below-market options.

**Strategic objectives:**
- Create a Homeowners Bill of Rights (by end of 2027)
- Rewrite the zoning ordinance (by end of 2027)
- Create a Housing Action Plan including review of former Housing Navigator plans (by July 2026)
- Implement the Housing Action Plan (by end of 2027)

**Target:** Tabor has stated Portsmouth needs ~1,500 units in 2 years to balance the market.

entries.title: Progress Portsmouth
entries.body:
Portsmouth's primary pro-housing advocacy organization. Founded and run by Gerry Duffy. Operates as an outside watchdog — not affiliated with city government, not elected, not funded by developers.

**Mission:** Push Portsmouth city government to actually implement housing reform. "Be the teeth."

**Key output — the 137-item HAP framework:**
- Version 2.4 as of March 18, 2026
- 137 action items across 13 categories
- Sources: comparable city plans (Keene, South Portland, Burien), Blue Ribbon Committee work plan, Planning Board recommendations, Places to Live Dialogue, 2022 RKG Housing Market Study, Byron Matteo GIS analysis
- Status: 7 complete, 16 in progress, 6 early stage, 108 not started
- Council voted 9-0 Feb 17, 2026 to use this framework as the basis for the official Housing Action Plan
- Public at: progress-portsmouth-tools.vercel.app/housing/hap-explorer.html
- GitHub: github.com/gduffy44-svg/progress-portsmouth-tools

**The 13 categories:**
Zoning & Land Use (33) | Permitting & Process (11) | Incentives & Financial Tools (20) | Housing Trust Fund & Funding (3) | Public Land & Property (6) | Partnerships & Capacity (9) | Preservation & Anti-Displacement (11) | Special Populations (6) | Education & Outreach (10) | Infrastructure & Services (5) | Production Targets & Data (11) | Climate & Sustainability (3) | Governance & Accountability (3)

**Key data points embedded in the framework:**
- 85.8% of Portsmouth residential land zoned exclusively single-family
- 69.9% of parcels in single-family zones don't meet current lot size requirements
- Multifamily zones generate 5.71x more assessed value per acre than single-family zones
- Vacancy rate 1.86% vs. 4-6% functional market threshold
- RKG projects demand for 3,124 additional units, 52% at ≤80% AMI

**Tools and resources:**
- HAP Explorer: progress-portsmouth-tools.vercel.app/housing/hap-explorer.html
- Housing Pipeline (32 tracked projects): progress-portsmouth-tools.vercel.app/housing/housing-pipeline.html
- Plain English Zoning Guide (362 pages translated)
- ADU Guide, Co-living Development Guide, Rental Market Snapshot, Housing Meetings Tracker

**Partner organizations:** Home For All (homeforall-seacoast.org), Workforce Housing Coalition of the Greater Seacoast (seacoastwhc.org), YIMBY Action

**Key framing:** "Portsmouth's zoning rules make it illegal to build the housing we already have." The Lincoln Avenue townhouses are the example — an excellent housing type that would require a zoning variance to build today.

**Website:** progressportsmouth.com

entries.title: Rockingham Regional Planning Commission (RPC)
entries.body:
Regional planning agency covering 27 Seacoast communities including Portsmouth. Based in Exeter at 156 Water Street. Phone: 603-778-0885. Website: therpc.org

**Portsmouth's RPC Commissioners:** Beth Moreau, Deaglan McEachern, John Tabor, Peter Britz (alternate). Three of Portsmouth's most important housing figures represent the city at the regional table.

**Key housing output — 2023 Regional Housing Needs Assessment (RHNA):**
- Required by NH RSA 36:47(II) — statutory obligation
- Conducted by Root Policy Research
- Available free at therpc.org/RHNA (full report + 7 appendices)
- Most relevant: Appendix C (Quantitative Data Tables), Appendix E (Fair Share Tables by municipality)

**Portsmouth fair share housing need (RHNA Appendix E):**
- By 2025: 680 total (463 owner, 217 renter, 52 renter <60% AMI)
- By 2030: 1,250 total (849 owner, 401 renter, 96 renter <60% AMI)
- By 2035: 1,635 total (1,104 owner, 531 renter, 127 renter <60% AMI)
- By 2040: 1,850 total (1,239 owner, 611 renter, 146 renter <60% AMI)

Portsmouth's 1,250-unit need by 2030 is the second largest in the region (behind Salem at 1,714). Combined with RKG's 3,124-unit demand projection and 1.86% vacancy rate, this is a third independent data source confirming severe undersupply.

**Important caveat:** The RHNA fair share table cannot be used directly to determine compliance with the Workforce Housing Statute (RSA 674:58-61) — that requires a separate municipal-level analysis.

**Services RPC provides Portsmouth:**
- General planning technical assistance including zoning ordinance review
- Transportation planning (MPO)
- Regional transit support via COAST
- Flood hazard and climate resilience work
- Route 1 Corridor Plan implementation
- GIS mapping assistance

**Regional context:** Towns like Greenland, Rye, and Newington lack meaningful mechanisms to build affordable housing — Portsmouth bears a disproportionate share of the regional burden.

**Schools myth-busting:** NH Housing commissioned "From Homes to Classrooms" (Urbanomics, Sept 2024) which debunks the claim that new housing raises school taxes. Smaller multifamily and ADU units generate fewer school-age children per unit than single-family homes, and net tax revenue from new housing typically exceeds marginal school cost increases.

entries.title: Portsmouth Housing Action Plan — 137-Item Framework
entries.body:
Progress Portsmouth built a 137-item Housing Action Plan framework that the council voted 9-0 on Feb 17, 2026 to use as the basis for the official plan. The Housing Action Plan is due July 2026 as a City Council strategic objective.

**Framework details:**
- 137 action items across 13 categories (Version 2.4, March 18, 2026)
- Status: 7 complete, 16 in progress, 6 early stage, 108 not started
- Sources: comparable city plans, Blue Ribbon Committee, Planning Board, Places to Live Dialogue, RKG 2022, Byron Matteo GIS analysis
- Public at: progress-portsmouth-tools.vercel.app/housing/hap-explorer.html

**PHC role:**
- Portsmouth Housing Committee (PHC) will triage and prioritize the 137 items
- PHC can push items to Planning Board or Planning Dept. depending on type
- PHC reports recommendations to council every 2 weeks
- Goal: keep council accountable to actually implementing the plan

**The 13 categories:**
Zoning & Land Use (33) | Permitting & Process (11) | Incentives & Financial Tools (20) | Housing Trust Fund & Funding (3) | Public Land & Property (6) | Partnerships & Capacity (9) | Preservation & Anti-Displacement (11) | Special Populations (6) | Education & Outreach (10) | Infrastructure & Services (5) | Production Targets & Data (11) | Climate & Sustainability (3) | Governance & Accountability (3)

**Housing Pipeline:** Progress Portsmouth tracks 32 active projects from early concept through occupancy at progress-portsmouth-tools.vercel.app/housing/housing-pipeline.html — updated continuously.

**Key data from the framework:**
- 85.8% of Portsmouth residential land zoned exclusively single-family
- 69.9% of single-family zone parcels don't meet current lot size requirements
- Multifamily zones generate 5.71x more assessed value per acre
- Vacancy rate 1.86% vs. 4-6% functional market threshold
- RKG projects demand for 3,124 additional units, 52% at ≤80% AMI

entries.title: Key Research Documents — Portsmouth Housing Reference
entries.body:
A curated index of the most important housing research documents relevant to Portsmouth.

**Local:**
- 2022 RKG Housing Market Study — Primary Portsmouth demand analysis. portsmouthnh.gov
- Places to Live Dialogue Report-Out — Feb 2024 joint council/planning board session
- Portsmouth Zoning Guide (Plain English) — Progress Portsmouth v3. progressportsmouth.com
- 2025 Master Plan Update — 18-month process, underway. portsmouthnh.gov/planportsmouth/master-plan
- HAP Explorer — Progress Portsmouth 137-item tracker. progress-portsmouth-tools.vercel.app

**Regional:**
- 2023 Rockingham Regional Housing Needs Assessment — therpc.org/RHNA
- 2024 Assessment of Housing Supply — NH Dept of Business and Economic Affairs
- 2024–25 Biennial Housing Plan — NH Housing Finance Authority
- 2024 Residential Rental Cost Survey — NH Housing (annual statewide rent data)
- From Homes to Classrooms — Urbanomics for NH Housing, Sept 2024 (debunks housing/school tax myth)

**National:**
- Out of Reach 2025: New Hampshire — NLIHC. NH housing wage: $35.08/hour
- State of the Nation's Housing 2025 — Harvard Joint Center for Housing Studies
- The Gap: New Hampshire — NLIHC affordable housing shortage data by state
- Housing in NH: Shortage Raises Costs — NH Fiscal Policy Institute, Jan 2025

**Key stat:** A worker needs to earn $35.08/hour to afford a modest two-bedroom apartment in NH without spending more than 30% of income. At minimum wage that requires working 2.4 full-time jobs (Out of Reach 2025, NLIHC).
